exemplar.title: An investigation into how perceived effort affects the perceived quality of certain paintings: A replication of Kruger et al.

detailedExemplarData.title: An investigation into how perceived effort affects the perceived quality of certain paintings: A replication of Kruger et al.

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and wellâstated investigative aim with relevance explained through concrete everyday examples
Accurate description of DualâProcess Theory and effort heuristic with explicit link to investigation
Hypotheses clearly state and operationalize both IV and DV
weaknesses: Minor awkwardness in wording slightly reduces clarity
Limited depth on cognitive processing stages in theory description
Ambiguity remains over coding of âpreferential choiceâ responses
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three introduction strands reached the 5â6 band with explicit aim, model description, and operationalized variables. Minor imprecision in wording and depth justifies awarding a 5 rather than full marks.

strengths: Design choice (independentâsamples) is explained with reference to demand characteristics
Sampling technique described and justified, with participant characteristics and bias acknowledged
Controlled variables and materials are not only listed but their selection is explained
weaknesses: Could compare to alternative designs to strengthen rationale
Selfâselection justification could be deepened with comparison to stratified methods
Random allocation by arrival order may introduce bias
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three exploration strands meet the 3â4 band. The student explains design, sampling, and variable controls thoroughly, warranting the full 4 marks.

strengths: Descriptive and inferential statistics are appropriately chosen and largely accurate
Graphical presentation directly addresses the hypothesis with correct labels
Statistical computations are consistent with raw data
weaknesses: Interpretation of findings is contradictory, claiming both support and null acceptance
Graph lacks error bars and reporting of effect size
Minor formatting and reporting inconsistencies in statistical results
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (statistics and graph) sit in the 5â6 band, but interpretation is inconsistent and only meets the 3â4 descriptor. A balanced holistic score of 4 reflects this mixture of high technical skill and partial interpretive weakness.

strengths: Strengths and limitations are clearly identified and linked to the investigation
Proposed modifications are justified and tied to specific limitations
weaknesses: Findings discussion is brief and conflates non-significance with theoretical support
No consideration of study power or sample size in evaluation
Ethical implications (e.g., deception) are not addressed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While strengths, limitations, and modifications fully satisfy top-band descriptors, the findings discussion is underdeveloped (3â4 band). A holistic score of 3 reflects these mixed qualities.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart is provided that directly represents the hypothesis (choice frequency by effort condition).  Labels and title communicate DV and IV, and the graph aligns with the data table.  Minor stylistic shortcomings (no error bars, non-zero baseline clarity) warrant a 5 rather than 6.

Findings are stated and linked to the hypothesis, but interpretation is partially contradictory (claims both âsupports heuristicâ and ânull acceptedâ).  The student recognises non-significance yet attempts theoretical linkage, indicating partial but inconsistent interpretationâcharacteristic of the 3â4 band; a mid-band 3 is awarded.

Both research and null hypotheses are stated.  The IV (stated time of creation) and DV (preferential choice of painting) are clearly operationalised, fulfilling all criteria for the highest band.  Slight ambiguity over whether âpreferential choiceâ constitutes a binary or ordinal variable keeps the score at 5.

Self-selected sampling is described and reasons for its use and its potential bias are given, showing explanatory depth.  Participant characteristics and inclusion/exclusion criteria are also covered.  The strand therefore sits solidly in the 3â4 band; the completeness merits the upper value 4.

Dual-Process Theory and the effort heuristic are accurately summarised, key researchers are cited, and the concepts are explicitly connected to the present investigation of artwork quality.  While the description lacks depth on cognitive processing stages, the required âdescription + explained linkâ is present, satisfying the top bandâagain with some minor imprecision, so a 5 rather than a 6.

The student references Dual-Process Theory and effort heuristic when discussing results, but the narrative is brief and muddled (equating non-significant test with âsupportâ).  This is more than a mere restatement but lacks depth of discussion, fitting the 3â4 band at a 3.

The student formulates a clear aim (to investigate whether perceived creation time influences perceived quality of paintings) and explicitly links this to everyday use of effort heuristics (e.g., teachers grading).  The everyday relevance is more than a brief mention; it is explained through concrete examples, so the strand fits the 5â6 descriptor.  Minor wording awkwardness prevents awarding the full 6.

Multiple strengths and limitations are explained and explicitly related to the investigation (independent design reduces demand characteristics; self-selection threatens representativeness, etc.).  The explanations show cause-and-effect reasoning, satisfying the top band; minor omissions (ethical points) keep it to 5.

Several controlled variables are listed with explicit rationale (quiet room, random allocation, standardised instructions).  Materials are described and reasons for their selection (same artist to avoid style confound) are provided, fulfilling the âexplainedâ criterion.  Coverage is thorough enough for a 4.

Each limitation identified is paired with a concrete modification (use stratified sampling, re-word question to improve activation of heuristic).  The suggested changes are justified and clearly linked, meeting the descriptor for 5â6 marks; limited methodological detail on feasibility holds it at 5.

The student identifies an independent-samples design and justifies it (avoiding demand characteristics/order effects).  This moves beyond description into explanation, matching the 3â4 band.  Details of alternative designs or deeper methodological rationale are limited, so a 3 is appropriate.

Appropriate descriptive (mode, variation ratio) and inferential (chi-square for nominal independent data) statistics are applied and calculations appear internally consistent with the raw table.  Minor reporting issues (formatting, unnecessary decimal) prevent awarding the maximum 6 but accuracy is largely sound.

annotations.comment: Final statement correctly restates null result; add reflections on study power and sample size to round out evaluation.
The introduction succinctly outlines Dual-Process Theory but would benefit from deeper elaboration on how Systems 1 and 2 interact in effort heuristic judgments.
The replication of Kruger et al. is well described, but the student should discuss any deviations from the original methodology and their impact on validity.
The transition from background theory to investigative aim is clear, linking effort heuristic relevance to everyday grading scenarios.
The aim is clearly stated and operationalized to IBDP students; refine wording to specify exactly how âperceptual qualityâ will be quantified.
Independent variable is defined by time categories but student should clarify whether this is treated as a binary categorical variable in analysis.
Dependent variable operationalization is concise; consider adding how responses are coded and if any rating scales were pretested.
Hypotheses are appropriately stated; explicitly note the expected effect size or directionality for clarity in inferential testing.
Research design rationale is well explained with respect to demand characteristics; could be strengthened by comparing to a within-subjects alternative.
Sampling method is described but the student should justify self-selection against stratified alternatives to reduce representativeness bias.
Participant characteristics are thoroughly described with exclusion criteria; consider including a demographic table for clarity.
Control of environment is well justified; student could mention how timing consistency (4 pm) guards against diurnal mood effects.
Materials selection of same-artist paintings reduces confounds; suggest justifying slide display duration beyond two minutes for cognitive load consistency.
Procedure description is comprehensive, but random allocation âby arrival orderâ could lead to systematic bias; propose true random number assignment.
Consent and debriefing steps reflect ethical considerations; student should clarify how anonymity was maintained in raw data reporting.
Descriptive statistics are correctly computed using mode and variation ratio; results interpretation should reference why mode was chosen over mean/median.
The narrative claims both support and rejection of heuristic effects; student must reconcile contradictory interpretations in findings discussion.
Graph axes are labeled clearly and reflect hypothesis structure; inclusion of error bars or confidence intervals would strengthen inferential insight.
The chi-square test choice is appropriate for nominal, independent groups; reporting degrees of freedom and effect size (Ï) would improve completeness.
Inferential result is accurately reported as non-significant; student should discuss implications of p = 1 and consider power limitations.
Discussion briefly links findings to Dual-Process Theory but conflates non-significance with support; student should distinguish theoretical trend from statistical conclusion.
Strengths and limitations are explicitly linked to the design and sampling; consider adding ethical reflections (e.g., deception impact) for completeness.
Modification suggestions are well justified in relation to limitations; strengthen by proposing a clear plan for implementing stratified sampling.
Procedure modification identifies question rewording; student should pilot alternative phrasing to confirm activation of effort heuristic.
The conclusion overstates significance at p â¤ .01 despite non-significant results (p = 1); correct the claim to reflect null acceptance.
